title: NZ Holiday Parks — The Complete Family Guide 2026
meta_desc: Everything about NZ holiday parks for families: TOP 10 vs independents, powered vs unpowered, freedom camping rules, costs, and the best parks by region.
intro: New Zealand's holiday park network is genuinely world-class — clean, safe, well-equipped, and often in spectacular locations. A family of 4 can stay in a quality holiday park cabin for $120–200/night, or park a campervan on a powered site for $60–90/night. Here's what you need to know.

rows: Full kitchen, living area, private bathroom
Motel standard in a park setting

sections.heading: TOP 10 Holiday Parks vs Independent Parks
list: TOP 10 is a membership network of ~55 parks across NZ. Consistent quality standards — you know what you're getting.
TOP 10 Clubcard ($55/year): saves 10% at all TOP 10 parks. Pays for itself in 2–3 nights.
Independents can be excellent (or terrible). Read recent reviews carefully. Facilities vary enormously.
TOP 10 parks tend to have better playgrounds, pools, and kid-specific facilities.
For spontaneous travel, TOP 10 parks in popular areas book up fast — always call ahead in peak season.
Some independent parks in remote areas (West Coast, Fiordland) are the only option — and many are excellent.

sections.heading: Facilities to Look For with Kids
list: Playground: essential for young kids. Check it's fenced if you have toddlers.
Heated swimming pool: many TOP 10 parks have indoor pools — a rainy day lifesaver.
Camp kitchen: well-equipped camp kitchens save significant money. Check stove/oven quality.
Laundry: with kids, you'll need this every 3–4 days minimum.
Drying room: vital on the West Coast and in Fiordland where it rains often.
TV/games room: when kids hit the wall. Many TOP 10 parks have one.
Distance to toilets: cabin allocation matters. Ask for a site close to bathrooms for night trips with kids.

sections.heading: Freedom Camping — The Rules
sections.body: Freedom camping (sleeping in your vehicle outside commercial parks) is legal in NZ only for certified self-contained vehicles. Self-contained means: toilet, grey water tank, and 3+ days of fresh water storage. Non-self-contained vehicles CANNOT legally freedom camp. Fines are $200–400. Check freedomcamping.org.nz for legal sites near your route.
list: Download the CamperMate app: shows all freedom camping sites, holiday parks, and dumps stations on one map.
Self-contained certificate (CSC): campervans hired from major operators (Jucy, Maui, Britz) are certified. Budget van rentals may not be.
Some councils ban freedom camping entirely (Queenstown Lakes District is very strict — $400 fines are common).
DOC (Department of Conservation) campsites: basic but beautiful. $8–15/person/night. Book at doc.govt.nz.
Never freedom camp on private land without permission. Respect is what keeps these spots available.
